Correct. A virtual destructor matches the C++ rule being tested by this prompt.

prompt: What must be true for `dynamic_cast<D*>(bptr)` to work?

options.text: Base must be polymorphic (have at least one virtual function)
options.explanation: Correct. `dynamic_cast` downcast through a base pointer requires a polymorphic base type (typically any virtual function, often a virtual destructor).

options.text: D must have a virtual destructor
options.explanation: Not required. `D` does not need a virtual destructor for the cast itself to compile/work.
Not required. `bptr` may be null; `dynamic_cast` on null pointer yields null.
Incorrect. Disabling RTTI prevents this runtime checked cast mechanism.
Incorrect. Cast is constrained by actual runtime type and inheritance relationship.

explanation: For `dynamic_cast<D*>(bptr)`, the source base type must be polymorphic (have at least one virtual function) so RTTI is available for runtime checked downcast. If the cast fails, the result is `nullptr` (for pointer casts).

prompt: What does `final` on a virtual function mean?

options.explanation: Overload still possible by signature, but not override.

options.text: It cannot be overridden in derived classes
options.explanation: Correct. It cannot be overridden in derived classes matches the C++ rule being tested by this prompt.
Incorrect. This statement does not match the C++ rule being tested in this prompt.
Already virtual.
Incorrect. This statement does not match the C++ rule being tested in this prompt.

explanation: Core rule: It cannot be overridden in derived classes. This is the best answer for the specific code/rule tested by this question.

prompt: What is the key effect of declaring a base-class destructor as `virtual`?

options.text: It makes all methods virtual automatically
options.explanation: Only functions declared virtual are virtual.

options.text: It enables correct destruction through base pointers
options.explanation: Correct. It enables correct destruction through base pointers matches the C++ rule being tested by this prompt.

options.text: It forbids stack allocation of derived classes
options.explanation: No such restriction.
A virtual destructor alone does not make class abstract.
Virtual functions require runtime dispatch metadata.

explanation: Core rule: It enables correct destruction through base pointers. This is the best answer for the specific code/rule tested by this question.

options.explanation: Virtual dispatch calls the most-derived override.
Correct. Through a base pointer, virtual dispatch calls `Derived::f`.
Only one call to `f()` occurs.
Code is valid.
Dispatch itself is well-defined.

explanation:
Polymorphism requires virtual functions in the base type.
Example:
Base& r = derivedObj;
r.f(); // dispatches to most-derived override

options.explanation: `rd` refers to `Derived`, so virtual dispatch uses `Derived::name`.
`rb` refers to a real `Base` object.
Correct. Each call dispatches based on the dynamic object referred to by each reference.
Valid code.
No UB here.

explanation:
Base references preserve polymorphic behavior.
Example:
Base* p = &d;
std::cout << p->name(); // Derived

options.explanation: During base construction, virtual dispatch does not call derived overrides.
Correct. In constructors/destructors, virtual calls dispatch to the current class (`Base` here), not the most-derived override.
Only one `speak()` call is made (from `Base` constructor).
The code is valid C++.
This behavior is defined by the language.
Initializer-list order does not control member initialization order.
Correct. Members are initialized in the order they are declared in the class.
The order is specified by the standard.
No such rule exists.
Not optimization-dependent.

prompt: When an object goes out of scope, in what order are its local variables and members destroyed?

options.explanation: Destruction order is reverse of construction/initialization order.
Correct. Automatic objects are destroyed in reverse order; class members are destroyed in reverse declaration order.
Order is defined, not random.

options.text: Only base classes are destroyed; members are not
options.explanation: Members are destroyed too.

options.text: From smallest type to largest type
options.explanation: No size-based rule exists.

prompt: What is the primary language-level difference between `struct` and `class` in C++?

options.explanation: False. Both `struct` and `class` can have member functions, constructors, operators, etc.
False. Both support inheritance.

options.text: Default member/base access differs (`struct` public, `class` private)
options.explanation: Correct. `struct` defaults to `public` members and bases; `class` defaults to `private`.

options.text: `struct` is a C-only feature and not valid modern C++
options.explanation: False. `struct` is fully supported in modern C++.

options.text: `class` objects are heap-only while `struct` objects are stack-only
options.explanation: False. Storage duration is independent of `struct` vs `class`.

explanation:
The core language difference is default access. Everything else (methods, templates, inheritance, constructors, etc.) works in both.
Example:
struct S { int x; }; // x is public by default
class C { int x; };  // x is private by default
